# RustySNES — APU (SPC700 / S-SMP + S-DSP) Supplemental Research

**Generated:** 2026-06-24 · Immutable supplement to `research-report.md`. Do not edit; new findings → new dated file.

Covers the audio subsystem and — most importantly — the **asynchronous clock-domain resync model**
that is the SNES emulation accuracy crux. Every nontrivial claim is cited.

---

## 1. Hardware facts

- **SPC700 (S-SMP) clock = 1.024 MHz**, "independent from the rest of the SNES, and may drift
  slightly with temperature." Generated from a **24.576 MHz ceramic resonator** (the same resonator
  clocks the S-DSP, internally at 3.072 MHz). ([SNESdev S-SMP](https://snes.nesdev.org/wiki/S-SMP))
- **Resonator tolerance ±0.5% (5000 ppm).** Measured per-console S-DSP sample rates span
  **32036–32152 Hz** (nominal 32000 Hz), warming slightly. The variable S-CPU↔S-SMP communication
  delay + this drift is a documented cause of **TAS desyncs on real hardware**. ([undisbeliever — S-SMP clock](https://undisbeliever.net/blog/20250313-smpspeed.html))
- **S-DSP:** 8 voices, **BRR-compressed samples** with **4-point Gaussian interpolation**, 32 kHz
  16-bit stereo, "1 stereo sample every 768 resonator cycles." ([SNESdev S-SMP](https://snes.nesdev.org/wiki/S-SMP))
- **64 KiB ARAM** (two 32K×8 PSRAM), time-shared "1 S-SMP access for every 2nd S-DSP access."
  ([SNESdev S-SMP](https://snes.nesdev.org/wiki/S-SMP))
- **Three timers:** two @ 8 kHz, one @ 64 kHz. ([SNESdev S-SMP](https://snes.nesdev.org/wiki/S-SMP))
- **Four communication ports:** SMP side `$F4–$F7` ↔ CPU side `$2140–$2143`. "8 stored values,
  each is a one-way communication written from one side, and readable only from the other side" —
  each port is two latches (CPU→SMP, SMP→CPU). ([SNESdev S-SMP](https://snes.nesdev.org/wiki/S-SMP))
- **IPL boot ROM:** the SMP starts executing a 64-byte IPL that handshakes over the ports to receive
  the audio program uploaded by the main CPU at boot — a timing-sensitive handshake the emulator
  must reproduce.

## 2. The async-resync model (the accuracy crux)

Because the SPC700/S-DSP run from a separate crystal, a faithful emulator runs them as a
**separate timeline** and forces lockstep with the main CPU **only when the two communicate** — a
read/write of the four ports `$2140–$2143` (or vice versa).

The reference emulators (higan/bsnes/ares, Near/byuu) use a **cooperative-threaded scheduler**:
each processor is a coroutine (libco) holding its own clock; a single signed **relative-time
counter** expresses how far ahead one processor is. When the CPU steps N of its clocks it
**subtracts N × 24,576,000**; when the SMP steps N it **adds N × 21,477,272** — the two source
frequencies as integer scaling factors, so the counter is exact (no floating point).
`Thread::synchronize(other)` resumes `other` until its clock catches up to the caller **before** any
cross-processor access, so the SMP can run arbitrarily far ahead as long as neither side touches the
ports; a sync is **forced once per scanline** to bound audio latency.
([byuu.net — Designing a scheduler](https://byuu.net/design/schedulers/),
[bsnes.org — Cooperative threading](https://bsnes.org/articles/cooperative-threading/),
[higan/sfc/cpu/timing.cpp](https://github.com/byuu/higan/blob/master/higan/sfc/cpu/timing.cpp))

### The RustySNES port (faithful + deterministic)

Use a **single-threaded master-clock scheduler** (the CPU/PPU/HDMA advance in lockstep on the
21.477 MHz timebase) with the SPC700/S-DSP on their own 1.024 MHz timebase tracked by a
**relative-time accumulator** (the ×24576000 / ×21477272 trick, or the equivalent reduced rational
ratio). The bus resyncs the SMP up to "now" on:

1. every CPU access to `$2140–$2143` (and SMP access to `$F4–$F7`), and
2. once per scanline (latency bound).

This keeps the determinism contract (the accumulator is integer/exact; no host RNG or wall-clock
leaks into the core) while modeling genuine asynchrony. The coroutine technique is the **conceptual
model**, not the literal implementation — Rust coroutines fit awkwardly and complicate
save-state/netplay determinism; the accumulator-on-a-single-thread approach gets the same accuracy
deterministically.

### Determinism caveat

Real-hardware SPC resonator drift (±0.5%) is **intentionally NOT modeled** in the deterministic
core — the SPC domain uses a fixed nominal 1.024 MHz. Offer drift only as a separate
non-deterministic "hardware-accurate audio" toggle, never in the bit-identical path. RTC chips
(S-RTC, RTC-4513) likewise must be seeded/frozen.

## 3. Validation oracle (see test-ROM section of the main report)

- **SingleStepTests/spc700** (MIT) — per-instruction JSON + bus activity, the clean SPC700 CPU
  oracle. ([github.com/SingleStepTests/spc700](https://github.com/SingleStepTests/spc700))
- **blargg `spc_*` ROMs** (`spc_dsp6`, `spc_mem_access_times`, `spc_spc`, `spc_timer`) — the
  **cycle-accurate SPC700 + S-DSP oracle**; print a running per-opcode hash. **Mesen-S is the only
  emulator that passed all of them.** License unstated → external/reference tier only.
  ([NESDev forum — blargg SPC ROMs](https://forums.nesdev.org/viewtopic.php?t=18005))
- **blargg `snes_spc` library** (LGPL-2.1) — the de-facto cycle-accurate S-DSP audio reference;
  use as an **external `.spc`→`.wav` comparison oracle only, never vendored** into the MIT/Apache
  core. ([github.com/blarggs-audio-libraries/snes_spc](https://github.com/blarggs-audio-libraries/snes_spc))
- **gilyon/snes-tests** (MIT) — covers SPC-700 opcodes (all addr modes, except SLEEP/STOP) with
  golden `tests*.txt` tables; the committable layer. ([github.com/gilyon/snes-tests](https://github.com/gilyon/snes-tests))

No JSON single-step oracle exists for the S-DSP itself (it is streaming/stateful, not
instruction-discrete) — DSP accuracy is validated by **audio-output comparison** vs blargg `snes_spc`
or ares/bsnes.
